objectives: Define abstraction as removing or hiding detail irrelevant to a problem, leaving only what is needed to solve it.
Distinguish representational abstraction from generalisation/categorisation, and abstraction from decomposition.
Apply abstraction to an unseen scenario by justifying which real-world details are kept and which are discarded.

retrieval.q: What does abstraction remove or hide from a problem?
retrieval.a: Unnecessary detail that is irrelevant to solving the problem, leaving only the relevant information.

retrieval.q: Name the two types of abstraction in 2.1.1.
How does abstraction differ from decomposition?
retrieval.a: Abstraction removes/hides unnecessary detail; decomposition breaks a problem into smaller sub-problems.
Breaking a problem into smaller sub-problems, each able to be solved as a self-contained sub-procedure.

retrieval.q: Interleaving (2.1.2): why might a satnav cache a computed route?
retrieval.a: To reuse an expensive result quickly without recomputing it, speeding up response and reducing load.

keywords.def: Removing or hiding detail not relevant to the problem so only useful information remains.
Simplifying one specific thing into a model of just the relevant detail, e.g. a road network as a graph.
Grouping many things by their shared characteristics into one reusable model, e.g. an Account class.
A simplified representation of reality keeping only the detail needed for the problem.
Breaking a problem into smaller sub-problems; distinct from abstraction, which removes detail.
A point in a graph abstraction representing a place or object, e.g. a junction in a road network.
A connection in a graph carrying a value such as distance or time between two nodes.
Information that affects the problem's solution and so must be kept in the model.

content.heading: What abstraction is
points: Abstraction removes or hides detail that is not relevant to the problem.
It leaves a simpler model that is faster to build, run and maintain.
The skill in the exam is justifying why discarded detail is irrelevant to this specific problem.

content.heading: Representational abstraction
points: Simplifies one specific thing into a model, e.g. a road network becomes a graph of nodes and weighted edges.
The London Underground map keeps line order and interchanges but discards true geography.
Discarded detail (scenery, surface) does not affect the task of choosing a route.

content.heading: Generalisation / categorisation
points: Groups many things by their shared characteristics into one reusable model.
Example: one Account class capturing what savings and current accounts have in common.
Makes programs easier to extend — a new case reuses the existing general model.

content.heading: Abstraction vs decomposition (common trap)
points: Abstraction removes/hides unnecessary detail to simplify.
Decomposition breaks a problem into smaller sub-problems.
They are different skills — never blur them in an answer.

content.heading: Exam technique
points: Name the specific detail you keep AND the detail you discard.
Justify why each discarded detail is irrelevant to this problem.
Generic definitions score poorly; the marks are for applied reasoning.

worked_example.title: Abstracting a 'car' for a top-down racing game
steps: Identify the problem: model a car well enough to race it from a top-down view.
Keep: position/coordinates on the track — needed to draw and move the car.
Keep: speed/velocity and heading/direction — needed to simulate movement and handling.
Discard: the engine's chemistry, exact paint colour and VIN.
Justify the discard: none of these affect how the car behaves or is driven in the race, so they add complexity without value.
State the type: this is representational abstraction — one specific thing simplified into a workable model.

task: A hospital wants software to manage patient appointments. Using abstraction, design a model of a 'patient'. Name three pieces of data the model should KEEP, two real-world details it should DISCARD, and justify why each discarded detail is irrelevant to the appointments problem.
challenge: A weather simulation models continuous air as a discrete 3-D grid of cells. Explain why this is an act of abstraction, what detail it discards, and one consequence (good or bad) of choosing a coarser grid.

exit_ticket.q: A studio models a road network as nodes and weighted edges. Which type of abstraction is this, and what do the edge weights represent?
exit_ticket.a: Representational abstraction; the edge weights represent the cost of travelling between junctions, e.g. distance or time.

exit_ticket.q: Why is using one Shape type for circles and squares an example of generalisation?
exit_ticket.a: It groups different things by their shared characteristics into a single reusable model, so new shapes can reuse it.

exit_ticket.q: A student says abstraction means breaking a problem into sub-problems. Correct them.
exit_ticket.a: That is decomposition; abstraction removes or hides irrelevant detail to simplify the model.
